questions.q: In the GPU rendering pipeline, which stage transforms 3D vertex positions into screen coordinates?
questions.explanation: The vertex shader executes on each vertex and applies transformations to convert 3D coordinates into screen space. The rasterizer converts vertices into fragments but doesn't apply coordinate transforms directly.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the rasterization stage in the rendering pipeline?
questions.explanation: Rasterization converts vector primitives (triangles) into discrete pixel fragments. Vertex transformations happen in the vertex shader, lighting can occur in vertex or fragment stages.

questions.q: Which component of the rendering pipeline determines the final color of each pixel?
questions.explanation: The fragment shader runs per-pixel and outputs the final color through calculations like texturing and lighting. The vertex shader only transforms positions; clipping removes out-of-bounds primitives.

questions.q: What does the clipping stage remove from the rendering pipeline?
options: Pixels that fail the depth test
Triangles facing away from the camera
questions.explanation: Clipping removes primitives (or portions of primitives) that fall outside the view frustum boundaries. Back-face culling handles camera-facing tests; depth test occurs later in per-fragment operations.

questions.q: In the z-buffer algorithm, what value indicates that a pixel is closest to the camera?
questions.explanation: Z-buffer uses depth values where smaller (closer to camera) values are kept. A minimum depth means the point is nearest; maximum depth indicates far distance from the viewpoint.

questions.q: Which stage in the rendering pipeline operates on individual fragments after rasterization?
questions.explanation: Per-fragment operations (depth test, blending, stencil test) run after rasterization on each pixel fragment. Primitive assembly groups vertices into triangles before rasterization.

questions.q: What is the output of the rasterizer before the fragment shader executes?
questions.explanation: The rasterizer produces fragments with interpolated vertex attributes (colors, normals, texture coords). The fragment shader then processes these to determine final pixel colors.

questions.q: In homogeneous coordinates, a 3D point (x, y, z) is represented as a 4D vector for what reason?
options: To enable perspective division and matrix transformations
questions.explanation: Homogeneous coordinates (x, y, z, w) allow affine transformations and perspective division through matrix multiplication. The w-component enables proper perspective correction during rasterization.

questions.q: When does perspective division occur in the rendering pipeline?
questions.explanation: Perspective division occurs after the vertex shader outputs homogeneous coordinates but before rasterization. The GPU automatically divides by w to convert to screen space coordinates.

questions.q: What is the relationship between primitive assembly and rasterization in the pipeline?
options: Primitive assembly converts triangles into fragments; rasterization transforms them
Rasterization groups vertices into primitives; assembly converts to pixels
Primitive assembly groups vertices into triangles; rasterization converts them to pixels
They are sequential stages with no data passing between them
questions.explanation: Primitive assembly takes vertex data and groups it into geometric primitives (triangles). Rasterization then converts those triangles into pixel fragments. They form a direct pipeline sequence.
